survival_reason: The removed __syncthreads() at line 130 is redundant because synchronization is already guaranteed by the __syncthreads() at line 127 (the last barrier in the reduction loop). After the reduction completes, all threads have already synchronized and computed the same mean value from shared_sum[0]. The second pass reads only thread-local mean (a register) and writes to separate shared_sq_sum locations (or the same array but different indices), so there's no data race. Since the kernel already enforces synchronization before the mean computation, removing this barrier does not change program semantics.
kill_strategy: No input can expose a difference because the barrier removal does not affect correctness. The mean is computed from shared_sum[0] after the reduction loop's final synchronization (line 127). All threads read the same fully reduced value before proceeding, regardless of the removed barrier. Even with different values, shapes, or block configurations, the redundant barrier cannot cause observable divergence.
recommendations: Improve the kernel by removing redundant __syncthreads() calls to avoid confusion. For test coverage, stress tests should vary block sizes (non-power-of-two) and spatial_size values to catch real synchronization bugs (e.g., reduction stride issues), not just input values. The test suite should also consider launching kernels with different grid/block dimensions to expose warp-divergence or shared-memory aliasing issues.

robustness_suggestion: The kernel lacks proper synchronization validation and has redundant barriers that mask potential race conditions. The defensive practice missing is ensuring that all threads synchronize at critical computation boundaries, particularly between phases where shared memory is reused. A better approach would be to: 1) Use consistent synchronization patterns with clear comments explaining each barrier's purpose, 2) Separate shared memory arrays for mean and variance computations to prevent any potential conflicts, and 3) Add assertions or checks that ensure proper synchronization when shared memory is repurposed. This makes the kernel's synchronization requirements explicit and robust against accidental barrier removal.
